item.title: AI is Breaking Two Vulnerability Cultures
item.description: A week ago the Copy Fail vulnerability came out, and Hyunwoo Kim immediately realized that the fixes were insufficient, sharing a patch the same day. In doing this he followed standard procedure for Linux, especially within networking: share the security impact with a closed list of Linux security engineers, while fixing the bug quietly and efficiently in the open. His goal was that with only the

item.title: Cartoon Network Flash Games
item.description: Explore a collection of Cartoon Network browser games inspired by The Powerpuff Girls, Dexter’s Laboratory, Samurai Jack, and other fan-favorite series.

item.title: Google Broke reCAPTCHA for De-Googled Android Users
item.description: The company that decides whether you're a bot now also requires you run its software to prove otherwise.

item.title: Code has always been worthless | Blog
item.description: For forty years we pretended software was the product. SaaS moved the price to the service. AI moved it past zero. The artefact is free now — what's worth paying for is knowing which code to write.
item.author: (Ivan Roganov)

item.title: When is your birthday? - The Math Behind Hash Collisions
item.description: The mathematics behind why encryption works — and why it sometimes doesn’t.

item.description:
By Mr. Market

Brand has historically been considered an imprecise, flailing effort, only necessary once you hit a certain size and even the...

item.title: Human Typing Habits and Token Counts
item.description: Normal human habits like swapped letters, fillers, shorthand, pasted IDs, boundary whitespace, and nearby word forms can change token count without changing intent much.

item.title: Humanity is self-deprecating
item.description:
A year ago agents were good, but comparing them today is a folly. Today, agents effectively replace junior developers and companies have realized that.
I ...

item.title: Pair Your Coding Agent with a Reviewer
item.description: The simplest improvement you can make to your agentic programming workflow is to run two agents instead of one. One writes code in its own worktree; the other, in a parallel worktree, reviews it. They share a task list and talk after every TDD cycle. The reviewer is the holder of the lamp: it keeps the end goal fresh in context across the whole task, catching drift before it accumulates.

item.title: Engineering Intelligence from Autocomplete
item.description: LLMs don't think; they predict the next token. Discover the engineering tricks from RAG to Tree of Thoughts that force a simple autocomplete to behave intelligently.
item.author: (Marton Antal Szel)

item.title: My first corrupted hard drive problem
item.description: How I had to deal with a corrupted hard drive on a production server.

item.title: Can LLMs model real-world systems in TLA+?
item.description: Editors’ note: AI has been actively pushing the frontier of applied formal methods for computing systems. In this article, the Specula team wrote about their experience of evaluating LLMs on modeling system code, the basic capability for agentic model checking, using TLA+, a specification language f

item.title: My Response to Open Web Censorship Calls-to-Action | Scott Larson
Fixing Markdown Syntax Is a Nightmare | mdview.io
item.description: Explore common Markdown syntax issues and solutions, focusing on rendering challenges across various dialects and fixing LaTeX and Mermaid errors effectively.

item.title: AI makes weak engineers less harmful
item.description: Like other kinds of puzzle-solving, software engineering ability is strongly heavy-tailed. The strongest engineers produce way more useful output than the average, and the weakest engineers often are actively net-negative: instead of moving projects along, they create problems that their colleagues have to spend time solving. That’s why many tech companies try to build a small, ludicrously well-paid team instead of a large team of more average engineers, and why so far this seems to be a winning strategy.

item.title: Beyond DevOps — Readables
item.description: On letting Claude run my infra, and what I saw on the other side.

item.title: Building a Harper Desktop App
item.description: We're dedicating the next month to just one thing: getting a Harper desktop app shipped.

item.title: Zed is pretty nice | blog.kronis.dev
item.description: My blog, where I attempt to collect my thoughts and share the occasional interesting topic with others

item.title: You gave me a u32. I gave you root. (io_uring ZCRX freelist LPE) · ze3ter
item.description: Linux 6.15 shipped a new zero-copy receive subsystem for io_uring called ZCRX. It manages a pool of network I/O vectors (niovs) using a stack: freelist[] holds available slot indices, free_count is the depth. There is no upper bound check on free_count. Two separate kernel teardown paths both return niovs to the same freelist, and when they overlap, free_count exceeds the allocated array length. The result is a 4-byte out-of-bounds write into adjacent slab memory.

item.title: C++ Style and Technique FAQ
item.description: Modified February 26, 2022

item.title: Crystal Gazing
Google Cloud Fraud Defence is just WEI repackaged
item.description: In May 2026, Google announced “Google Cloud Fraud Defense - the next evolution of reCAPTCHA.” The announcement described a QR code challenge where users scan a

item.title: taken.
item.description: A web page that tells you what your browser gave away the moment you arrived. No login, no form, no permission. Most pages do this. None of them tell you.
item.author: (Matt Wheeler)

item.title: Introduction | Meshtastic
item.description: Discover Meshtastic: a community-driven, open-source project using LoRa radios for long-range, off-grid communication.

item.title: OpenAI's WebRTC Problem - Media over QUIC
item.description: Media over QUIC: There are ways to do voice AI without being traumatized by WebRTC.

item.title: HomePod mini feels like magic, but it's just good timing
item.description: Apple introduced the HomePod mini six years ago, in 2020. I'm not one into smart speakers, but the feature that made me take a closer look was their ability to form stereo pairs, without any direct wired connection. I know there are other speaker manufacturers with wireless speakers, but to my knowledge, Apple was just using AirPlay over WiFi... so how does it work? Through the magic of buying two HomePods mini (pictured above), I found out. A video detailing the process is embedded below:

item.title: Hi stranger
item.description: I'm at home, sitting on the kitchen table. I just took my boys to school and I'm about to start my work. I'm writing this message directly to you. And you are reading it. Hello!
